Based on the reviewed literature discussing methods to prevent post-operative wound infections and the previous discussion highlighting the significance of stringent hand hygiene in preventing transmission between patients, I would argue that option **D** - practicing proper hand washing continues to stand out as the most effective initial step. The evidence supports this view because although advanced methodologies exist (such as negative pressure therapy or specialized wound dressings), they either lack conclusive evidence proving their superiority over simpler measures or introduce logistical difficulties unrelated specifically targeting hand-to-mouth/eye routes of pathogen dissemination central issue here involving simultaneous admissions multiple compromised hosts needing close surveillance constant vigilant monitoring compliance with established protocols necessary ensure minimal disruptions normal routines of everyday life ongoing therapeutic regimens prescribed physicians overseeing case loads heavy workloads demand unwavering attentiveness consistency applying correct technique correctly at right intervals throughout shifts continuous training reinforcement feedback cycles foster improvement retention knowledge skillsets required implementing sound policies procedures encouraging active participation multidisciplinary teams fostering culture accountability transparency reporting robust tracking metrics evaluating performance trends facilitating timely corrective actions mitigation risks safeguarding patients' well-being above all else.
